1.pericope_label: Woe to the Shepherds of Israel
1.synthesis: God pronounces woe on the shepherds of Israel “who have been feeding yourselves! Should not shepherds feed the sheep?” (v.2). They eat the fat and clothe themselves with the wool but do not strengthen the weak, heal the sick, bind up the injured, bring back the strayed, or seek the lost; instead they rule “with force and harshness” (vv.3–4). So the sheep are scattered and become food for wild beasts (vv.5–6). God therefore declares himself “against the shepherds” and will rescue his flock from their mouths (vv.7–10). The integrative thread is God’s indictment of self-serving leaders who exploit rather than tend the flock: Israel’s kings, priests, and prophets have fed themselves at the sheep’s expense, and God, the true Owner of the flock, holds them accountable and removes them — exposing that leadership is a stewardship of care, not a privilege of self-gain.
1.historical_context: “Shepherd” was a standard ancient Near Eastern title for kings and rulers (Jeremiah 23:1–2; Psalm 78:71–72), so the indictment targets Israel’s failed kings and leaders whose misrule led to the exile. The catalogue of pastoral neglect (v.4) — failing the weak, sick, injured, strayed, and lost — describes the very opposite of true shepherding, and the scattering of the flock (v.5) is the dispersion among the nations. God’s declaration “I am against the shepherds” (v.10) announces their removal from office.
1.literary_structure: This opening section (vv.1–10) launches the great shepherd chapter, indicting the false shepherds before God announces himself as the true Shepherd (v.11). The contrast between shepherds who “feed themselves” (v.2) and the care the flock needed (v.4) sets up the whole chapter’s movement from human failure to divine rescue.
1.theological_application: Spiritual and civil leaders are stewards of a flock that belongs to God, accountable to feed and protect rather than exploit (v.2; 1 Peter 5:2–3). The marks of false shepherding — neglecting the weak and lost while consuming the flock’s resources for self (vv.3–4) — remain a searching test for every pastor, elder, and ruler. God’s declaring himself against such shepherds (v.10) assures the abused flock that their Owner sees and will act, and warns leaders that their office is given for the good of others, with a reckoning to come.
1.christology: Against the shepherds who fed themselves (v.2) stands Jesus, “the good shepherd” who “lays down his life for the sheep” (John 10:11) — the very opposite of the hireling who flees (John 10:12–13). He seeks the lost, binds the injured, and gathers the scattered, fulfilling God’s pledge to shepherd the flock himself. As the chief Shepherd (1 Peter 5:4) he will hold under-shepherds to account, and as the slain-yet-living Lamb he is both the Shepherd and the sacrifice who rescues the flock from the mouths of all who would devour them.

11.pericope_label: I Myself Will Search for My Sheep
11.synthesis: God declares, “I myself will search for my sheep and will seek them out” (v.11). As a shepherd seeks his scattered flock, he will rescue them from all places where they were scattered on a day of clouds and thick darkness, bring them into their own land, and feed them on the mountains in good pasture (vv.12–14). “I myself will be the shepherd of my sheep… I will seek the lost, and I will bring back the strayed, and I will bind up the injured, and I will strengthen the weak” (vv.15–16). The integrative thread is God’s personal assumption of the shepherd’s office the false shepherds abandoned: the very tasks they neglected — seeking, gathering, healing, strengthening — God now pledges to do himself, the emphatic “I myself” making the rescue of the flock a matter of his own direct and tender action.
11.historical_context: God’s pledge to seek out the scattered (vv.11–12) promises the regathering of the exiles from “the day of clouds and thick darkness” — the catastrophe of 586 BC — back to the land and its good pasture (vv.13–14). The point-by-point reversal of the shepherds’ failures (v.16; cf. Ezekiel 34:4) shows God personally doing all that they refused. The shepherd-and-flock imagery resonates with Psalm 23 and Isaiah 40:11.
11.literary_structure: This central section (vv.11–16) turns from indictment to divine promise, the emphatic repeated “I myself” (v.11, v.15) marking God’s personal intervention. The list of restorative actions (v.16) deliberately mirrors and reverses the neglect catalogued in Ezekiel 34:4, structuring the chapter as failure answered by faithfulness.
11.theological_application: When human shepherds fail, God himself takes up the care of his people — “I myself will be the shepherd” (v.15). This is the believer’s deepest security: the flock’s welfare ultimately rests not on fallible leaders but on God’s own seeking, gathering, and healing love (v.16; Psalm 23:1). The God who seeks the lost and strengthens the weak meets his people precisely in their scattering, darkness, and injury. We are called to entrust ourselves to this Shepherd, and those who lead are reminded that they shepherd only under and on behalf of the One who owns the sheep.
11.christology: God’s promise “I myself will search for my sheep” (v.11) is fulfilled in the incarnation of the Son, who declared “I am the good shepherd” (John 10:11) and told of the shepherd who leaves the ninety-nine to seek the one lost sheep until he finds it (Luke 15:4–6). In Christ, God came in person to do what no human shepherd would — to seek and save the lost (Luke 19:10), to bind the brokenhearted, and to gather his scattered flock into one (John 11:52), shepherding them to springs of living water (Revelation 7:17).

17.pericope_label: I Will Judge Between Sheep and Sheep
17.synthesis: God turns to judge within the flock, “between sheep and sheep, between rams and male goats” (v.17). The strong have fed on the good pasture and trampled the rest, drunk the clear water and muddied the remainder, and shoved the weak with horn until they were scattered (vv.18–21). God will save his flock and judge between the fat and the lean (v.22), and will “set up over them one shepherd, my servant David… and I, the LORD, will be their God” (vv.23–24). The integrative thread is judgment within the flock and the promise of the one Davidic Shepherd: God not only removes oppressive leaders but addresses oppression among the sheep themselves, defending the weak against the strong, and crowns the rescue by appointing the messianic “David” to shepherd a flock of which the LORD himself is God.
17.historical_context: The judgment “between sheep and sheep” (v.17) addresses oppression within the covenant community — the strong exploiting the weak (vv.18–19) — not merely the failure of rulers. The promise of “my servant David” (v.23), centuries after David’s death, is a messianic prophecy of a coming Davidic king (Jeremiah 30:9; Hosea 3:5), echoing the covenant of 2 Samuel 7:12–16. The covenant formula “I will be their God” (v.24) seals the relationship.
17.literary_structure: This section (vv.17–24) extends the shepherd theme inward, judging the flock itself before introducing the messianic shepherd. The contrast of fat and lean sheep (v.20) gives way to the climactic promise of the “one shepherd, my servant David” (v.23), bridging the divine shepherding of the prior section with the Davidic shepherd to come.
17.theological_application: God’s justice reaches not only oppressive leaders but oppression among his people — the strong who consume more than their share and trample the weak (vv.18–21). He defends the lean against the fat (v.22), warning the privileged within the community against pushing aside the vulnerable (1 Corinthians 11:21–22). The promise of one Davidic shepherd (v.23) directs the believer’s hope beyond every human leader to the Messiah under whom the flock finds justice, and calls us to share the pasture rather than muddy the water for others.
17.christology: “My servant David” who will shepherd the one flock (vv.23–24) is Jesus, the Son of David and the one Shepherd of the one flock (John 10:16), the root and offspring of David (Revelation 22:16). He judges between sheep and goats at the last day (Matthew 25:31–33), vindicating the oppressed weak whom the strong despised. In him the covenant promise “I will be their God” is fulfilled as Immanuel, God with us, the Davidic King in whom God himself shepherds and reigns over his people forever.

25.synthesis: God promises “a covenant of peace” that will banish wild beasts so the flock may dwell secure even in the wilderness (v.25). He will make them and the places around his hill a blessing, sending showers of blessing; the trees will yield fruit and the land its increase, and they will be secure, no longer plundered or a prey (vv.26–29). “They shall know that I… am with them… And you are my sheep, human sheep of my pasture, and I am your God” (vv.30–31). The integrative thread is the covenant of peace that crowns the shepherd chapter with comprehensive shalom: under the Davidic shepherd, God pledges security, fruitfulness, freedom from fear, and his abiding presence — a restored Eden-like flourishing in which the relationship “I am your God” is fully realized, all flowing from his covenant commitment rather than the flock’s deserving.
25.historical_context: The “covenant of peace” (v.25) is the new-covenant promise of restored shalom (Isaiah 54:10; Ezekiel 37:26), banishing the wild beasts that symbolize hostile nations and dangers (Leviticus 26:6). The “showers of blessing” and fruitful land (vv.26–27) reverse the covenant curses of drought and barrenness (Deuteronomy 28). The closing covenant formula (vv.30–31) restates the heart of the relationship between God and his flock.
25.literary_structure: This closing section (vv.25–31) crowns the shepherd chapter with the covenant of peace, moving from rescue and rule to comprehensive blessing. The imagery of security, showers, and fruitfulness (vv.26–27) culminates in the covenant formula “I am your God” (v.31), sealing the whole chapter’s movement from woe to peace.
25.theological_application: God’s covenant of peace promises his people not merely rescue but comprehensive flourishing — security from fear, provision, and above all his presence: “I am with them… I am your God” (vv.30–31). This shalom is the gift of grace under the shepherd-king, not the wages of merit, assuring the believer that the God who rescues also keeps and blesses. The promise that the flock will dwell secure even in the wilderness (v.25) teaches that true peace is found in God’s presence and covenant, even amid a hostile world (John 16:33).
25.christology: The “covenant of peace” (v.25) is established by Christ, who “himself is our peace” (Ephesians 2:14) and who, as the great Shepherd of the sheep, was brought again from the dead “by the blood of the eternal covenant” (Hebrews 13:20). Through his cross he made peace, reconciling his flock to God (Colossians 1:20) and securing the showers of blessing of the Spirit. In him the covenant formula reaches its consummation, when God will dwell with his people and be their God forever (Revelation 21:3).
